coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and nitrite based on silver/carbon nanocomposite synthesized by carbon dots as reductant via one step method
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Si
Liu, Xiaoying
Huang, Na
Lu, Qiujun
Liu, Meiling
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a new multifunctional electrochemical platform for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and nitrite (NO2 −) based on silver/carbon nano-composite (Ag/C NC) was firstly proposed. Ag/C NC was synthesized by using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reductant by a facile one-step synthesis method and confirmed by UV-Vis, Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering. The Ag/C NC based electrodes showed excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the reduction of H2O2 and the oxidation of NO2 −. When Ag/C NC was dropped on the bare GCE, the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 occurred and showed wide linear range from 5.00μM to 11.05mM with the detection limit of 0.51μM. When Ag/C NC was dropped on graphene (GR) modified electrode, the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE showed extremely fast direct electron transfer and good electrocatalytic activity toward oxidation of nitrite. It showed wide linear range from 4.00μM to 2.00mM with the detection limit of 0.48μM. The Ag/C NC/GR/GCE was successfully applied in analysis of nitrite in real samples and gained good testing recovery. Therefore, the proposed electrochemical sensors for H2O2 and NO2 − exhibited many advantages, such as operational simplicity, rapid responses, good reproducibility and stability, and can be simply tuned to utilize for electrochemical detection of H2O2 and NO2 −. This study will open a facial and simple synthesis method for preparing carbon based noble metal nanocomposite and utilizes them in detection for different purposes, which may provide new opportunity and prospect in the field of electrochemical biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Ag/C NC
Hydrogen peroxide
Nitrite
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and real sample analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: state key laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21305042 , 21375037 ), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department ( 14B116 ), the Science and Technology Department ( 14JJ4030 , 13JJ2020 ), the Opening Fund of state key laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics , Hunan University ( 2013017 ), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University ( KLCBTCMR2011-08 ) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province FO Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle ( ST201422007 ), and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of electrochemical sensor for detection of H2O2 and nitrite.
TEM images of C-dots (A), Ag/C NC (B); insert: the HRTEM image of single Ag/C NC. And the UV spectra (C) and FTIR spectra (D) of C dots (a) and Ag/C NC(b).
CVs (A) and electrochemical impedance spectra (B) of bare GCE (a), C-dots/GCE (b) and Ag/C NC/GCE (c) in 2mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− at a scan rate of 50mV/s.

(A) CVs of bare GCE (a), C-dots/GCE (b), AgNPs/GCE (c) and Ag/C NC/GCE (d) in N2-saturated 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS in the presence of 3mM H2O2 and Ag/C NC/GCE (e) in the absence of H2O2 at the scan rate of 50mV/s. (B) CVs of Ag/C NC/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS in the presence of various concentrations of H2O2 (a: 2mM, b: 3mM, c: 4mM).

(A) CVs of bare GCE (a), GR/GCE (b), Ag/C NC (c) and Ag/C NC/GR/GCE (d) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS in the presence of 2.0mM NO2 − at scan rate of 100mV/s. (B) CVs of Ag/C NC/GR/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS in the presence of various concentrations of NO2 − (a: 0mM; b: 1mM; c: 2mM; d: 3mM).

(A) Amperometric responses of Ag/C NC/GCE upon the successive addition of H2O2 into N2-saturated 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS with the applied potential of −0.2V under stirring condition. (B) Plots of the reduction current vs. the concentrations of H2O2.

(A) DPVs of Ag/C NC/GR/GCE to different concentrations of NaNO2 (0μM, 4μM, 10μM, 30μM, 50μM, 75μM, 100μM, 150μM, 200μM, 250μM, 500μM, 750μM, 1000μM, 1500μM and 2000μM) and (B) calibration curve for NaNO2 determination in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) at the scan rate of 100mV/s.
Determination of nitrite in real water sample.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and nitrite based on silver/carbon nanocomposite synthesized by carbon dots as reductant via one step method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Science and technological innovation platform, Hunan Agricultural University, Hunan, Changsha 410128, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a new multifunctional electrochemical platform for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and nitrite (NO2 −) based on silver/carbon nano-composite (Ag/C NC) was firstly proposed. Ag/C NC was synthesized by using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reductant by a facile one-step synthesis method and confirmed by UV-Vis, Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering. The Ag/C NC based electrodes showed excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the reduction of H2O2 and the oxidation of NO2 −. When Ag/C NC was dropped on the bare GCE, the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 occurred and showed wide linear range from 5.00μM to 11.05mM with the detection limit of 0.51μM. When Ag/C NC was dropped on graphene (GR) modified electrode, the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE showed extremely fast direct electron transfer and good electrocatalytic activity toward oxidation of nitrite. It showed wide linear range from 4.00μM to 2.00mM with the detection limit of 0.48μM. The Ag/C NC/GR/GCE was successfully applied in analysis of nitrite in real samples and gained good testing recovery. Therefore, the proposed electrochemical sensors for H2O2 and NO2 − exhibited many advantages, such as operational simplicity, rapid responses, good reproducibility and stability, and can be simply tuned to utilize for electrochemical detection of H2O2 and NO2 −. This study will open a facial and simple synthesis method for preparing carbon based noble metal nanocomposite and utilizes them in detection for different purposes, which may provide new opportunity and prospect in the field of electrochemical biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, considerable attention has been paid to construction of sensors with high selectivity and sensitivity [1], as well as fabrication of simple and multifunctional sensors. The simplification requires to the material preparation and operational processes are simple; the multifunction requires to the realization of several different purposes on the same platform. Many photochemical sensors are developed in this respect [2,3]. Considering of multifunction or multipurpose, electrochemical sensors, especially enzyme-based amperometric sensors are of great necessity and have been widely studied. However, it is difficult to develop other multifunctional electrochemical sensors for their complicated preparation and modifying steps and expensive experimental apparatus [4], as well as it is difficult to find a suitable multifunctional material for this purpose.

Recently, the construction of non-enzymatic and multifunctional electrochemical sensors for some important life-related molecules has attracted much interest for researches. For example, H2O2 is an important byproduct in various biological processes [5,6]. It is necessary and vital to fast determination of H2O2 in various fields of bioanalysis as well as food security and environmental protection [7,8]. The electrochemical platforms based on gold nanoparticles-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots [9] and amorphous carbon-supported silver nanoparticles [10] and the turn-on fluorescence sensor based on MnO2-nanosheet modified upconversion nanosystem [11] for detection of H2O2 have been developed. Another important molecule is nitrite (NO2 −), which is one of the widespread inorganic pollutants existing in foods, environment and physiological systems [12,13]. Hence, effective determination of NO2 − is of great importance for human and animals health [14]. Recently, Nurul et al. [15] reported the electrochemical synthesis of graphene oxide-silver nanocomposite on GCE, and showed good electrocatalytic activity toward oxidation of nitrite. Abbas et al. [16] prepared gold nanoparticles-multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified carbon paste electrode, which displayed electro-catalysis to nitrite. Though many methods were used to detection of H2O2 and NO2 − separately, a few of the sensors that can realize for simultaneously sensing of the two substances using the same material. Recently, Yuan et al. [17] prepared the sensor based on polyethylenimine-bridged graphene oxide-gold film for electrochemical detection of the reduction of H2O2 and oxidation of NO2 −. Liu et al. [18] reported PdCo alloy nanoparticle-embedded carbon nanofiber for electroanalysis of H2O2 and NO2 −. Though their work exhibited high sensitivity and realized simultaneous detection, however, their work also exhibited some disadvantages, such as complicated material preparation process and high cost. Therefore, designing and developing of some novel and cheaper nano-materials for multifunctional electrochemical sensors are of great importance [5].

To date, metal nano-particles (M NPs) may be a good alternative for sensitive detection of various biomolecules, because of their catalytic activity and good electrical conductivity [19]. For catalytic applications, M NPs usually suffer from oxidation and aggregation issues, leading to the decrease of the catalytic activity [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to utilize new stabilizers or capping agents to protect M NPs from aggregation. Recent discovery suggested that carbon dots (C-dots) can be utilized as stabilizers or capping agent [10]. To the best of our knowledge, C-dots, which are one of the new members of carbon nanomaterials family, have gained enormous attention because of their excellent biocompatibility and stability [20,21]. Recently, our group has successfully synthesized C-dots by one-pot electrochemical carbonization of ethylene glycol [21]. The as synthesized C-dots possess excellent stability and reducibility without any further surface functionalization. It can act as the reductant for direct synthesis of AgNPs to obtain silver/carbon nanocomposite (Ag/C NC). This method is fast and simple without any additional reductant and stabilizing agent compared with other conventional synthesis method for AgNPs.

In this work, aimed to find a novel material for construction of multifunctional platform, Ag/C NC was synthesized by a simple method using the carbon dots as reductant. The Ag/C NC was successfully used for construction of electrochemical sensors for the reduction of H2O2 and oxidation of NO2 − with good electrochemical responses. To the best of our knowledge, the Ag/C NC was firstly used for the electrocatalysis of H2O2 and NO2 −. It may expand the strategy of the carbon dots in preparing metal nanoparticles to prepare carbon based metal nanomaterials in application in chemical and biosensing areas.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Graphene (GR) was purchased from XFNANO Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China) and used without further purification. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M) was prepared with Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and 0.1M Na2SO4. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (millpore-Q).

The UV–vis spectra (UV) were obtained on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra (IR) were collected on a Nexus-670 Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instrument Co, USA) with KBr pressed pellet transmission mode. The size and morphology of the nanoparticles was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Japan), dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, England) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Zeiss Sigma, Germany). Electrochemical experiments, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and chronoampermetry were carried out with a research electrochemical workstation of SP-150 (BioLogic Science Instruments, France) controlled by EC-Lab software. A three-electrode system was used which contained a glassy carbon electrode as working electrode, a saturated calomel reference electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a carbon rod electrode as the counter electrode. All potentials here were cited versus SCE. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

C-dots were electrochemically synthesized based on our previous work [21]. Briefly, ethylene glycol and sodium hydroxide solution were used as the precursor and electrolyte, platinum sheets were employed as the positive and negative electrodes. C-dots were synthesized by electrolyzing in the electrolyte solution under 30V for 40min. After electrolysis, the electrolyte solution was adjusted to neutral with hydrochloric acid and then dialyzed (Mw 1000) against water for two days to remove residual small molecules and salts, followed by centrifuging at 12000rpm for 10min to remove the precipitate particles.

The Ag/C NC was synthesized as follows: A certain amount of AgNO3 aqueous solution (48.56mM) was mixed with 1mL of the as-synthesized NaOH treated C-dots aqueous solution, the color of the mixture solution changed from yellow to light yellow at room temperature after 15min, indicating the formation of AgNPs (Scheme 1 A). In the presence of excess of C-dots, the AgNO3 was completely reduced. In order to obtain solid product for IR, TEM and SEM, the solution was dried at 45°C for 48h.

Glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) was firstly polished with emery paper and 50nm alumina slurries followed by rinsing thoroughly with ultrapure water. The electrodes were successively ultrasonicated in ethanol and water and then washed thoroughly with ultrapure water to obtain fresh and clear surface and dried in the air. The Ag/C NC modified electrode was fabricated by drop-casting of a certain volume of Ag/C NC solution on GCE and dried at room temperature. The modified electrode is directly used in the detection of H2O2 (Scheme 1A). For comparison, the AgNPs was prepared by sodium citrate as reductant, controlling the amount of silver nitrate precursor was the same as that of the C-dots as reductant. 5μL GR was dropped onto the clean electrode and dried in the air for 4h, then a certain volume of Ag/C NC was attached onto the surface of GR modified electrode and dried at room temperature for 5h. This modified electrode was used for electrochemical detection of nitrite, as show in Scheme 1B.

Fig. 1 A and 1B show the typical TEM images of the as-synthesized C-dots and Ag/C NC, it is obviously seen that the C-dots were of low multiple dispersity and these particles showed the diameters ranging from 2 to 4nm as the literature reported [21]. After reaction with AgNO3, the diameters of the as-prepared Ag/C NC ranged from 10 to 12nm (Fig. 1B, S1A). As shown in Fig. S1B, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was also used to measure the size distribution of Ag/C NC, which exhibiting the larger size of Ag/C NC with the average diameter of the Ag/C NC is about 18–24nm. This may result from the existence of certain hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the Ag/C NC, which make the hydrodynamic diameter larger than that coming from TEM [22–25]. And the Ag/C NC shows good dispersion without obvious aggregation with the dispersion coefficient (PdI) of 0.301 was measured by DLS. This may result from that the C-dots which can act as the reductant as well as the stabilizing agents to attach on the AgNPs surface through hydrogen bonding to protect AgNPs from aggregation [21]. Fig. S2 shows the SEM image (A) and EDS (B) of the Ag/C NC. It was found that Ag/C NC uniformly distributed on the silicon wafer on SEM image. EDS was used to analyze the chemical composition of Ag/C NC, and it was found that the peaks of C, O and Ag were obviously found as shown in Fig. S2B. Therefore, the above results showed that Ag/C NC was successfully prepared and C-dots were existed in Ag/C NC.

The UV–vis spectra of C dots (a) and Ag/C NC (b) are showed in Fig. 1C. The as-synthesized C-dots (a) showed a strong absorption peak at 260nm, which can be typically attributed to the π-π* transition [21]. After reaction with AgNO3, besides the strong absorption at 260nm, a broad peak at around 410nm (b) was observed, which is the typical surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak of AgNPs [10,26]. It is indicated that the formation of AgNPs and existing of residual C-dots in this solution. The successful preparation was further confirmed by FTIR. The result is shown in Fig. 1D, the characteristic peaks at 3408 and 1074cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of the hydroxyl groups of C-dots [21] (Fig. 1D, a). To improve and initiate the reduction ability of C-dots, the silver nitrate solution is mixed with the as synthesized NaOH treated C-dots aqueous solution on which the carbonyl groups would probably partly reduced to hydroxyl groups [27]. While comparing with those of the C-dots, the intensity of carbonyl groups (CO,1721cm−1) slightly decreased after reaction with AgNO3 and this is because the C-dots was partially oxidized when the AgNO3 was convert into AgNPs. The UV and FTIR results all suggested that the AgNO3 was successfully reduced into AgNPs and Ag/C NC was formed.

Electrochemical properties of different modified electrodes were investigated by CVs in 2mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− containing 0.1MK2SO4 solution. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a pair of well-defined redox peaks for Fe(CN)6 3−/4− was obtained at bare GCE (Fig. 2A, a). It is obvious that the peak current was the lowest on the C-dots modified electrode (Fig. 2A, b). This may result from the relative weak conductivity of C-dots. At the Ag/C NC/GCE (Fig. 2A, c), the peak current decreased in comparison with bare GCE, indicating that the electron transfer resistance increased somewhat and the conductivity of NC/GCE become smaller than that of GCE. These results were similar to the literature [28,29]. All results illustrated that the C-dots and Ag/C NC NC were immobilized on the electrode.

EIS is widely used to study the interface properties of electrode surfaces. As shown in Fig. 2B, the impedance plot for the bare GCE (Fig. 2B, a) exhibited a very small semicircle, which suggested a low electron transfer resistance. When C-dots were modified onto the bare GCE surface (Fig. 2B, b), the charge transfer resistance value increased dramatically, which showed poor electron transfer ability of C-dots. The resistance value of Ag/C NC/GCE (Fig. 2B, c) decreased obviously in comparison with C-dots/GCE. This result showed that Ag/C NC can facilitate electron transfer. These results were in agreement with the results from CVs.

The electrochemical catalysis ability of different modified electrodes toward H2O2 was studied by CVs in N2 saturated 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 3mM H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3 A, it can be obviously seen that the current responses toward the reduction of H2O2 are weak on both the bare GCE (Fig. 3A, a) and C-dots/GCE (Fig. 3A, b). There is an obvious peak on the AgNPs modified electrode (Fig. 3A, c), which may ascribe to the reduction of H2O2. From Fig. 3A (d), the Ag/C NC modified electrode exhibited a remarkable catalytic reduction peak current for H2O2 reduction, indicating that the higher electrocatalytic activity of Ag/C NC toward the reduction of H2O2. And in the absence of H2O2, Ag/C NC/GCE exhibited no current responses (Fig. 3A, e). As shown in Fig. 3B, the reduction peak current of H2O2 on Ag/C NC/GCE increased with concentrations of H2O2. These results showed that Ag/C NC exhibited good electrocatalytic toward the reduction of H2O2 and may be used for detection of H2O2.

The electrochemical oxidation of NaNO2 at different electrodes in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 2mM NO2 − was investigated by CVs. At the bare GCE (Fig. 4 A, a), a weak oxidation peak at about 0.8V was observed. After Ag/C NC (Fig. 4A, c) was modified on GCE, the oxidation peak intensity was obviously enhanced compared with that of the bare GCE. However, the oxidation of nitrite occurred at about 0.90V on the Ag/C NC/GCE, which was positively shifted about 100mV than that at GCE (Fig. 4A, a). It may indicate that the Ag/C NC/GCE exhibited electrocatalytic for the oxidation of nitrite, but the electro-conductivity of Ag/C NC is not good. In order to obtain better catalysis responses to oxidation of nitrite, GR was utilized. The GR was modified on GCE, and then Ag/C NC was dropped on the GR modified electrode. It can be found that the oxidation of nitrite occurred at about 0.78V (Fig. 4A, d) on the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE. The electrochemical properties and interface properties of Ag/C NC/GR/GCE was studied by CVs and EIS. As shown in Fig. S3A, at the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE (b), the peak current intensity was similar to that of GR/GCE (a), the capacitive current become small than GR/GCE. A small diameter of the semicircle was observed on the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE (Fig. S3B, b), indicating that the Ag/C NC/GR can promote the electron transfer process. Therefore, the Ag/C NC/GR may have better catalysis property than that of Ag/C NC. The oxidation peak current increased at Ag/C NC/GR/GCE (Fig. 4A, d) compared with GR/GCE (Fig. 4A, b) and the oxidation peak potential was negatively shifted about 122mV compared with that of the Ag/C NC/GCE, this may result from the synergistic effect of Ag/C NC and GR. As shown in Fig. 4B, in the absence of NO2 −, Ag/C NC/GR/GCE exhibited almost no current response (Fig. 4B, a). The oxidation peak current on Ag/C NC/GR/GCE increased with concentrations of NO2 − (Fig. 4B, b, c, d). These results indicate that Ag/C NC/GR possesses an excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of NO2 −. In the process of detecting of small molecules, the modified electrodes with good conductivity, as well as the material with excellent catalytic performance are important for obtaining excellent response. Therefore, though the conductivity of C-dots and the NC are not very good, the catalytic performance of the NC to H2O2 and NO2 − is excellent.

Fig. S4A and B shows the CV responses of Ag/C NC and Ag/C NC/GR modified GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 5mM H2O2 and 5mM NO2 − with the scan rate ranging from 10 to 150mVs−1, respectively. It is found that both the reduction peak currents for H2O2 (abbreviated as I pc) and oxidation peak currents for NO2 − (abbreviated as I pa) increased with the square root of scan rates (v 1/2). The regression equations of H2O2 and NO2 − were I pc =−28.24− 5.10·v 1/2 (R=0.9951) (Fig. S4C) and I pa =18.36+4.14·v 1/2 (R=0.9938) (Fig. S4D), respectively. The equations indicated that the electro-reduction of H2O2 and electro-oxidation of NO2 − on the surface of the electrodes were diffusion-controlled electrochemical processes.

To get the optimal experimental results, it is necessary to optimize of the experimental conditions. The influences of the volume of Ag/C NC dropped on the GCE or GR/GCE, the pH value of the buffer solution were investigated by CVs.

As shown in Fig. S5A, the reduction peak currents increased with the volume of Ag/C NC from 4 to 8μL and then decreased when the volume was over 8μL. Therefore, 8μL was the optimal volume for the sensor of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S5C, when the volume of GR was fixed at 5μL, the oxidation peak currents increased with the volume of Ag/C NC from 4 to 10μL and then decreased when the volume over 10μL. Therefore, when detection of NO2 −, 10μL Ag/C NC was used as the optimal volume.

The effect of pH value was further studied in detecting of H2O2 and NO2 −. As shown in Fig. S5B, the reduction peak currents of H2O2 increased with pH from 5.0 to 7.0, and then decreased in the range of 7.0 to 8.0. Therefore, pH 7.0 was the optimal pH value for detection of H2O2. Similarly, as shown in Fig. S5D, the oxidation peak currents increased with pH 5.0 to 7.0, and then decreased in the range of 7.0 to 10.0. Therefore, pH 7.0 was the optimal pH value for detection of NO2 −. This can be ascribed to the instability of nitrite anions in acidic media. In contrast, nitrite oxidation becomes more difficult when the pH is above 7.0, owing to the lack of protons [30].

The amperometric responses to H2O2 at the Ag/C NC/GCE were investigated by successively adding H2O2 into a continuous stirring N2-saturated PBS (0.1M, pH 7.0) at −0.2V. As shown in Fig. 5 A, it can be clearly seen that after the successive addition of different concentrations of H2O2, the reduction currents quickly reached stable value, indicating that it is a fast amperometric response behavior. Fig. 5 B shows the calibration curve of the sensor and the linear detection range is estimated to be 5μM to 11.05mM. The regression equation was I (μA)=−0.18 − 0.0006 c (μM) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9956 and the detection limit of 0.51μM (signal/noise=3).

Under the optimum conditions, the oxidation peak currents of various concentrations of nitrite on the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE were recorded by DPVs. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the oxidation peak currents increased with concentrations of nitrite in pH 7.0 PBS, and the peak currents were proportional to the concentrations of nitrite in the range from 4μM to 2mM (Fig. 6B). The regression equation was I (μA)=3.92+0.0179 c (μM) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9951 and the detection limit of 0.48μM (signal/noise=3). Furthermore, the linear ranges and detection limits of the proposed sensor were compared with previous reports, and the results were listed in Table S1. It was found that the proposed sensor based on Ag/C NC exhibited good performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the Ag/C NC modified electrode towards H2O2 in the presence of biological interferences was examined. As shown in Fig. S6A, Ag/C NC/GCE exhibited well defined amperometric response towards H2O2 (a). And the results indicated that UA, AA, DA did not interfere the determination of H2O2. Similarly, the impact of the interferences on nitrite sensor was also studied (Fig. S6B). Ag/C NC/GR/GCE exhibited well defined current response towards 1mM nitrite (a). No significant responses were observed for 100 fold excessive additions of interferences, indicating that the selectivity of the proposed sensor toward oxidation of NO2 − was acceptable.

The reproducibility and stability of the electrochemical sensor were performed by measuring the current responses of H2O2 and nitrite. And the relative standard deviation (RSD) of Ag/C NC/GCE and Ag/C NC/GR/GCE were calculated to be 3.86% and 4.18% for five different electrodes when detection of 2mM H2O2 and nitrite, respectively, suggesting good reproducibility of the sensors. The stability of the sensors is a key factor in developing of good electrochemical sensor. The sensors were stored at room temperature when not in use, and they retained 93.21% and 92.68% of their initial values when detection of 2mM H2O2 and nitrite after 14days of storage, indicating that good stability of the sensors.

In addition, in order to verify the reliability of the proposed method, the Ag/C NC/GR/GCE was applied to analysis of nitrite in water samples using the standard addition method in three spiked samples, respectively. Firstly, all water samples were filtered with a 0.22μm membrane. And then the water samples were added into pH 7.0 PBS. Different concentrations of standard nitrite solution were added in the diluted solution and the experiment was repeated five times utilizing five different electrodes. The results are illustrated in Table 1 . The recovery range was from 94.50 to 105.80% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) ranged from 1.88 to 4.16%. The results confirmed that the application of the sensor to measure nitrite in real samples is reliable.

In summary, a relatively simple and convenient method for preparation of Ag/C NC using carbon dots as the reductant was proposed. The electrochemical sensors for the detection of H2O2 and nitrite based on Ag/C NC modified electrodes were proposed for the first time. The results showed the Ag/C NC was a suitable material for multifunctional detection. When Ag/C NC was directly dropped on GCE, it showed excellent electrocatalytic property toward H2O2 with fast current response, wide linear range, high selectivity and acceptable stability. When Ag/C NC was dropped on GR, the modified electrode showed better response for oxidation of nitrite compared with that of Ag/C NC. And it was successfully used in electroanalysis of the nitrite in real samples and the results were satisfactory. Therefore, it may indicate that the preparation of carbon-based metal nanocomposites using C-dots as the reductant may provide a promising platform for electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305042, 21375037), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14B116), the Science and Technology Department (14JJ4030, 13JJ2020), the Opening Fund of state key laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2013017), Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR2011-08) and key laboratory of Jiangxi Province FO Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle (ST201422007), and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.06.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
